---
title: "1.1 The Importance of Backtesting"
subtitle: "PDF notes | Chapter 01. Backtesting and Automated Execution"

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    mainfont: "NotoSerifCJK-Regular.ttc"
    mainfontoptions:
      - "Path=/usr/share/fonts/opentype/noto/"
      - "FontIndex=2"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSerifCJK-Bold.ttc"
    sansfont: "NotoSansCJK-Regular.ttc"
    sansfontoptions:
      - "Path=/usr/share/fonts/opentype/noto/"
      - "FontIndex=2"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSansCJK-Bold.ttc"
    monofont: "MapleMono-NF-CN-Regular.ttf"
    monofontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Bold.ttf"
      - "ItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Italic.ttf"
      - "BoldItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-BoldItalic.ttf"
    mathfont: "latinmodern-math.otf"
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
include-in-header:
  text: |
    \addtokomafont{disposition}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{title}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{author}{\rmfamily}
    \addtokomafont{date}{\rmfamily}
---

# Why Backtesting Comes First

Backtesting is the process of feeding historical data into a trading strategy to
see how the strategy would have performed. That historical performance is useful
only when the backtest reproduces the decisions, prices, and constraints that a
live trading program would face.

This qualification is the main reason Chapter 1 begins with backtesting rather
than with strategy examples. A careless backtest can be worse than no backtest:
it can make a fragile strategy look profitable and push the trader toward real
losses. A useful backtest therefore has two jobs at once. It estimates
performance, and it exposes the implementation assumptions behind that estimate.

# Backtest Published Strategies Yourself

Independent backtesting matters even when a strategy comes from a trusted
publication. Published articles often simplify implementation details so that
the main idea remains readable. Those details may look minor on paper, but they
can decide whether the live strategy is profitable.

Order timing is one example of this sensitivity. A market-on-open order is not
the same as a market order sent shortly after the open. A futures strategy that
uses the S&P 500 may behave differently depending on whether the order is sent
near the 4:00 p.m. stock-market close or near the 4:15 p.m. futures close.
Price choice creates another implementation decision: a signal based on the bid,
the ask, or the last traded price can trigger at different times and costs.

Because these details are part of the strategy, the backtest must make them
explicit. The backtest is not merely a verification exercise; it is the place
where an informal trading idea becomes a fully specified trading rule.

# Connect Backtesting to Automated Execution

The same implementation discipline links backtesting to automated execution.
Ideally, the backtesting program should be close enough to the live execution
program that the same strategy logic can be reused with different data feeds:
historical data in the backtest and live market data in production.

This design goal reduces the gap between simulated performance and live
behavior. If the backtest uses one set of assumptions and the execution system
uses another, the reported performance belongs to a strategy that may never be
traded. When the two systems share the same logic, implementation details such
as order type, timestamp, venue, and signal timing become easier to inspect and
harder to forget.

# Use Backtesting to Find Pitfalls

Once the implementation details are explicit, the backtest can be examined for
strategy-specific pitfalls. A long-short equity strategy, for example, must ask
whether the short positions were actually borrowable at a reasonable size. An
intermarket futures pair must ask whether the two closing prices occur at the
same time. If these constraints are ignored, the historical result may describe
an impossible trade.

Most backtesting pitfalls have the same direction of error: they inflate the
reported performance relative to what could have been achieved. This tendency is
what makes pitfall analysis part of the core research process, not a cosmetic
review after the Sharpe ratio looks attractive.

# Treat Publication as the Start of True Out-of-Sample Testing

Backtesting a published strategy also creates a more honest out-of-sample test.
Before publication, an author may have adjusted parameters after seeing weak
validation results. That adjustment can quietly turn the claimed
out-of-sample period into another in-sample period.

After publication, the strategy is effectively cast in stone. New data that
arrive after that point can test the published rule without the same hidden
opportunity for revision. Poor post-publication performance does not prove that
the author was careless, but it does raise the possibility that the published
result depended on a limited historical sample.

# Follow the Scientific Method, But Keep the Boundary Clear

Backtesting is also a way to improve a strategy. The research process begins
with a hypothesis about an arbitrage opportunity or market inefficiency. The
backtest then confirms, weakens, or refutes that hypothesis. If the result is
not good enough, the researcher can revise the hypothesis and test again.

This iterative process is useful only when exploration is separated from final
validation. Small implementation changes, such as altering a lookback window or
entering at the open rather than the close, may materially change performance.
Those changes are legitimate during exploration. Once the rule is frozen,
however, the final validation period should not be used for further tuning.

# Review Checklist

- What trading hypothesis is the backtest testing?
- Which order type, timestamp, venue, and price field does the strategy use?
- Could the same strategy logic run on live market data?
- Are short-borrow limits, closing-time mismatches, and other strategy-specific
  constraints represented?
- Is the post-publication or walk-forward period kept separate from model
  revision?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [Chapter overview](../../index.qmd)
- Next: [1.2 Common Pitfalls of Backtesting](../02-common-pitfalls-of-backtesting/web-notes.qmd)
